risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Those eligible were listed in alphabetical order by surname. They were then selected randomly from the patient list, using a computer-generated random numbers table." Comment: This indicates a truly random sequence generation method (computer-generated random numbers table) was used to select participants, which meets the criterion for low risk of bias for random sequence generation. The later statement that participants "were then randomly assigned to one of the two groups" is consistent with random allocation, and the method is explicitly described.

Quote: "Written consent was obtained in a face-to-face interview and the participants were then asked by the principal researcher to draw a sealed opaque envelope, in which a number card indicated to which group they had been allocated (1 = psycho-education group and 2 = routine care group)." Comment: This explicitly describes a sealed opaque envelope used to reveal assignment, which is a concealment mechanism that prevents the recruiter from foreseeing the upcoming allocation.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A trained psychiatric nurse (group instructor) approached the patients in person..." and "Except for the principal researcher and the group instructor, all other clinic staffs were blinded to treatment allocation." Comment: Participants and personnel delivering the intervention were not blinded; the intervention was a clearly distinguishable psychoeducation/support group versus routine care, and the paper only reports blinding of other clinic staff and an independent outcome assessor. Because the outcomes included self-reported caregiver burden and family functioning, knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence responses and delivered care, so performance bias is high.

Quote: "An independent assessor (a research assistant) was trained to undertake measurements at Time 1, 2 and 3 using a set of questionnaires." Comment: The study explicitly states that outcome measurements were undertaken by an independent assessor, and the main outcomes were questionnaire-based caregiver and patient functioning/burden measures plus rehospitalisation data obtained from hospital/outpatient records. Because the assessor was independent and the record-based outcome is objective, knowledge of allocation is unlikely to have influenced outcome measurement.

Quote: "Analysis of data was on an intention-to-treat basis... All subjects, who completed the intervention or not, were followed up over a 12-month post-intervention period." Comment: The study explicitly states ITT analysis and follow-up regardless of completion. It also reports very low attrition: "Thirty-one subjects (91.2%) from the mutual psychoeducation and support group completed the program... participants... who dropped out or absented in more than four group sessions - psycho-education group (n = 3) and routine care group (n = 2), were evaluated at three times of outcome measurements. Reasons for dropout or discontinuation... were mainly: insufficient time to attend (n = 2), patient’s mental state worsened (n = 3), not interested (n = 2), and the only person taking care of patient (n = 2)." This suggests small, fairly balanced loss to follow-up with reasons not clearly differential across groups, so attrition is unlikely to bias the results.
